title: Authenticated Admin Header Injection via Unsanitized Feed Name in Content-Disposition
description: The ctf_export_settings_json() method in CTF_Support.php retrieves a feed_name from the database and uses it unsanitized in both an fopen() call to /tmp/ and in a Content-Disposition HTTP response header. If a feed name contains CRLF characters (\r\n), additional HTTP headers can be injected into the download response.
vulnerable_code: // Note: NO quotes around $filename in Content-Disposition

safe_counterpart.note: CTF_Global_Settings.php version of this same function uses php://memory (no filesystem write) and wraps filename in double-quotes in Content-Disposition header, making it safe.

attack_scenario: 1. Admin navigates to feed builder and sets a feed name containing CRLF: e.g., 'myfeed\r\nX-Injected: evil'
2. Admin (or attacker with admin credentials) requests: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=ctf_export_settings_json&feed_id=<id>
3. The response includes the injected header: 'X-Injected: evil'
4. In older PHP/web server combinations, CRLF header injection can lead to response splitting
limitations: Requires administrator-level access to set the malicious feed name in the first place
Modern PHP (7.3+) blocks header injection with CRLF via header() — throws a warning and ignores the extra line
Impact is limited to the downloading administrator's browser session
The CTF_Global_Settings.php duplicate of this function is safe
remediation: Sanitize feed_name before using it in headers and filesystem paths. Use sanitize_file_name() or basename() on the filename. Wrap the filename in double quotes in the Content-Disposition header, consistent with the CTF_Global_Settings.php implementation. Use php://memory instead of /tmp/ to avoid filesystem writes.
references: CWE-113: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers
